topics: Creating a theatre script based on an emotion
Designing costumes and make-up for characters
Giving and receiving feedback after a performance
subject: Arts

sections.heading: The Grand Quest Begins — Writing Your Script
sections.body: Every great wizard starts their quest with a plan — and your plan is your script! You will write a short conversation between two or three characters, all built around ONE emotion. Imagine two friends arguing before a cricket match — that whole scene is powered by the emotion of anger! Keep it simple, keep it real, and make sure your chosen emotion shines through every line.
keyPoints: Pick one emotion like joy, fear, anger, or excitement and build your whole story around it
Write a short conversation between 2 or 3 characters that clearly shows that emotion

sections.heading: Costume and Make-up — Dress Like a Hero
sections.body: In this realm of theatre, how your character LOOKS tells the audience who they are before they even speak! Just like how you can tell a villain in a TV show by their dark costume, your character's clothes and face paint send a powerful message. Design the costume and make-up that fits your character's personality — a grumpy old man might have a frown drawn on, while a happy king might wear bright colours!
keyPoints: Costume and make-up help the audience understand your character quickly
Make sure the look matches the emotion and personality of your character

sections.heading: Stage Design — Build Your Magical Realm
sections.body: Every dragon needs a castle, and every play needs a stage! Use your cardboard stage model to plan where things will be placed — like furniture, trees, or doors. Props are objects your characters use, like a cricket bat or a thali. Planning this before the actual show means no confusion when the real performance happens — just like a team planning their fielding before a match!
keyPoints: Use the cardboard model to decide where characters stand and where props are placed
Good stage design makes the story feel real and exciting for the audience

sections.heading: The Puppet Spell — Switch to Stick Puppetry
sections.body: Here is a cool trick — if you do not want to act on stage yourself, you can cast the puppet spell! Cut out your costume design drawing, paste a stick on the back, and boom — you have a stick puppet! Now use the same cardboard stage as your puppet theatre. It is like playing with action figures, but YOU are telling the story and doing all the voices!
keyPoints: Cut out your costume drawing and attach a stick to make a puppet character
The same cardboard stage model can be used as a puppetry frame

sections.heading: Feedback — The Power of the True Hero
sections.body: Real heroes and true artists do something very brave — they listen to feedback! After your performance, your class will discuss what worked and what did not. Did the audience understand the story? Were the costumes neat? Did the stage look good? Think of it like a cricket match review — even Virat Kohli watches his innings again to improve. Good feedback and not-so-good feedback are both your friends — they help you grow stronger!
keyPoints: Discuss the script, costumes, and stage design openly with your class after the show
Accept both positive and negative feedback calmly — it only makes you better next time

summary: Chapter 20 is the Grand Finale of your theatre quest — the moment where all your powers of scripting, costume design, stage planning, and performance come together in one big show! You write a script based on a single emotion, design costumes and make-up for your characters, plan your stage using a cardboard model, and even have the option to turn everything into a cool stick puppet show. After the performance, the whole class gives feedback on every part of the show — because every great artist listens, learns, and keeps creating. Remember, in this quest there are no wrong answers — only new ideas waiting to be explored. Have fun and keep creating!
